summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on the state of PC hardware in 2015, noting that while desktop CPU performance has only improved 30-40% since 2011, mobile devices have become nearly 10 times faster in the same period. He argues that while the PC may be 'over' in terms of exciting CPU gains, storage technology—specifically PCIe NVMe SSDs—represents a genuine bright spot with 3x performance improvements. The post explains why disk speed matters so much: SSDs attack the PC's worst-case performance scenario by dramatically reducing latency variability. Atwood provides a practical parts list for a Skylake-based build centered around the new M.2 NVMe storage standard, positioning PCIe SSDs as an important evolutionary step for desktop computing.
key_points: Desktop CPU single-core performance improved only 30-40% from 2011-2015, while mobile CPUs became nearly 10x faster
PCIe NVMe SSDs offer 3x performance improvement over SATA SSDs and 10x reduction in drive latency
SSDs matter because they address the PC's worst-case scenario—the massive variability between CPU cache access (nanoseconds) and disk access (milliseconds)
Traditional HDDs have 11 million times more latency variability than L1 cache; SSDs reduce this to 166,000x
The Skylake platform's key innovation is connecting storage directly to PCI Express lanes via M.2
money_quotes: When your mobile devices get 10 times faster in the span of four years, it's hard to muster much enthusiasm for a modest 1.3× or 1.4× iterative improvement in your PC's performance over the same time.
If the CPU registers are how long it takes you to fetch data from your brain, then going to disk is the equivalent of fetching data from Pluto.
What I've always loved about SSDs is that they attack the PC's worst-case performance scenario, when information has to come off the slowest device inside your computer.
SSDs provide a reduction in overall performance variability of 66×!
The fun part of building a PC is that it's relatively easy to swap out the guts when something compelling comes along.
I believe mainstream PCI express SSDs are another important step in the overall evolution of desktop computing. Or its corpse, anyway.
key_insight: While PC CPU improvements have stagnated, storage technology—particularly PCIe NVMe SSDs—offers genuinely compelling performance gains by attacking the system's worst bottleneck: disk latency variability.
